0:0.rendering: The vision concerning Judah and Jerusalem that Isaiah son of Amoz saw in the days of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah, kings of Judah.
0:0.note: This opening word "vision" links Isaiah to prophetic books like Obadiah and Nahum, signaling the whole book as one sweeping divine panorama rather than scattered sermons.

0:1.rendering: Hear, O heavens, and listen, O earth, for the LORD has spoken: "I reared children and brought them up, but they have rebelled against me."
0:1.note: This opening summons echoes the ancient treaty language of Deuteronomy 32, where heaven and earth witness Israel's covenant loyalty or betrayal.

0:2.rendering: The ox knows its owner, and the donkey its master's feeding trough, but Israel does not know, my people do not understand.
0:2.note: This Hebrew wordplay on "know" (yada) echoes Eden's intimate relational knowing, exposing Israel's shocking failure to recognize their own Creator.

0:3.rendering: Woe to the nation that is sinful, a people weighed down by guilt, offspring of evildoers, children who act corruptly. They have abandoned the Lord, they have despised the Holy One of Israel, they have turned away backward.
0:3.note: The four crushing titles pile up like a legal indictment, echoing the very language of rebellious sons in Deuteronomy 21.

0:4.rendering: Why do you persist in rebellion? The whole head is sick, and the whole heart faint.
0:4.note: The Hebrew word for "persist" echoes the root for "sick" in the next line, painting rebellion as a self-inflicted wound that spreads through the body politic.

0:5.rendering: From the sole of the foot even to the head, there is no soundness in it: bruises and welts and raw wounds, not pressed out, not bound up, not softened with oil.
0:5.note: The prophet paints Israel's rebellion as one untreated body, echoing how ancient wounds were bandaged with oil, a detail that later resurfaces in the Good Samaritan story.

0:6.rendering: Your land is desolate, your cities burned with fire. Foreigners devour your fields right before your eyes, and it has become like a wasteland overthrown by strangers.
0:6.note: The Hebrew word for "overthrown" (mahpekah) echoes the exact term used for Sodom's destruction in Genesis 19, turning Judah's judgment into an echo of that ancient catastrophe.

0:7.rendering: Daughter Zion is left like a shelter in a vineyard, like a hut in a cucumber field, like a city under siege.
0:7.note: The Hebrew paints Zion as a lonely, fragile booth in a harvested field, an image of total vulnerability that echoes across the prophets.

0:8.rendering: If the LORD of hosts had not left us a few survivors, we would have been like Sodom, we would have resembled Gomorrah.
0:8.note: The phrase "LORD of hosts" echoes the divine title that first appears at the end of the book of Judges, hinting that even in judgment God preserves a remnant.

0:9.rendering: Hear the word of the Lord, you rulers of Sodom. Give ear to the teaching of our God, you people of Gomorrah.
0:9.note: Isaiah shocks by addressing Judah's leaders and people with the names of the two cities God destroyed for wickedness.

0:10.rendering: What use to me is the multitude of your sacrifices? says the Lord. I have had enough of burnt offerings of rams and the fat of well-fed beasts. I take no pleasure in the blood of bulls, or of lambs, or of goats.
0:10.note: The Hebrew word for "had enough" echoes God's weariness with the flood generation, turning Israel's most sacred rituals into something that exhausts him.

0:11.rendering: When you come to appear before me, who asked this from your hand, this trampling of my courts?
0:11.note: The Hebrew word for "trample" (ramas) paints worshippers as cattle stomping sacred ground, an image that echoes God's earlier warning about not letting livestock trample Sinai's holy mountain (Exodus 19).

0:12.rendering: Stop bringing worthless offerings. Incense is an abomination to me. New moons and sabbaths, the calling of assemblies, I cannot endure iniquity and solemn gathering.
0:12.note: The Hebrew piles up ritual terms only to slam them with God's rejection, exposing how empty worship can cloak ongoing injustice.

0:13.rendering: Your new moons and your appointed feasts, my soul hates them. They have become a burden to me, I am weary of bearing them.
0:13.note: The Hebrew word for "hate" here is the same one used for rejecting something as worthless, echoing God's weariness in Genesis 6:6 with humanity's evil.

0:14.rendering: When you spread out your hands in prayer, I will hide my eyes from you. Even though you multiply your prayers, I will not listen. Your hands are full of blood.
0:14.note: The shocking wordplay in Hebrew links "spread out your hands" (in prayer) with hands "full of blood" (from violence and injustice), showing worship and oppression cannot coexist.

0:15.rendering: Wash yourselves, make yourselves clean, remove the evil of your deeds from before my eyes, stop doing evil.
0:15.note: The Hebrew word for "wash" echoes the ritual cleansing priests performed in the temple, turning personal repentance into a vivid, embodied act.

0:16.rendering: Learn to do good. Seek justice. Rescue the oppressed. Defend the orphan. Plead the case of the widow.
0:16.note: The Hebrew verbs here form a rapid-fire sequence of imperatives that echo the covenant's call to mirror God's own passion for the vulnerable, a theme that runs from Exodus to the prophets.

0:17.rendering: Come now, let us argue it out together, says the LORD. Though your sins are like scarlet, they shall become white as snow. Though they are red like crimson, they shall become like wool.
0:17.note: The courtroom language "let us argue it out" echoes ancient Near Eastern covenant lawsuits, turning judgment into an unexpected invitation to reconciliation.

0:18.rendering: If you are willing and listen, you will eat the good of the land.
0:18.note: The Hebrew word for "listen" here is the same as "obey," echoing the covenant choice in Deuteronomy 28.

0:19.rendering: But if you refuse and rebel, you will be devoured by the sword. For the mouth of the LORD has spoken.
0:19.note: The sword that devours echoes the covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28, turning the people's own rebellion into the instrument of their undoing.

0:20.rendering: How the faithful city has become a prostitute! She was full of justice, righteousness lodged in her, but now murderers.
0:20.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "faithful" (emunah) turning to harlotry echoes covenant language from Hosea, exposing how a city meant to embody God's justice has become a den of violence.

0:21.rendering: Your silver has become slag, your wine is mixed with water.
0:21.note: The Hebrew word for "mixed" (diluted) echoes the very first verse of Isaiah, where the people are called a "seed" gone bad, showing their corruption runs from the root.

0:22.rendering: Your princes are rebels, companions of thieves. Every one of them loves a bribe and chases after gifts. They do not defend the orphan, and the widow's case never reaches them.
0:22.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "sarim" (princes) and "sorerim" (rebels) exposes how Israel's leaders have become the very disorder they should restrain.

0:23.rendering: Therefore, the Lord Yahweh of hosts, the Mighty One of Israel, declares: "Ah, I will get relief from my adversaries, and I will avenge myself on my enemies."
0:23.note: The Hebrew word for "get relief" (nacham) echoes God's grieving "regret" over creation's corruption in Genesis 6:6,7, showing divine justice as both sorrow and resolve.

0:24.rendering: I will turn my hand against you. I will smelt away your dross as with lye, and I will remove all your impurities.
0:24.note: The Hebrew word for "smelt" (tsaraph) echoes the refining fire imagery used for Israel in the wilderness and later in Malachi, showing God's judgment as purifying love rather than mere destruction.

0:25.rendering: I will restore your judges as they were at the first, and your counselors as at the beginning. Afterward you will be called the city of righteousness, the faithful city.
0:25.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "first" and "beginning" echoes the creation language of Genesis, promising a new start for Jerusalem.

0:26.rendering: Zion will be redeemed by justice, and those in her who turn back will be redeemed by righteousness.
0:26.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "redeemed" (padah) echoes the exodus deliverance, now applied to a future restoration rooted in justice and righteousness.

0:27.rendering: Rebels and sinners will be shattered together, and those who abandon the Lord will be consumed.
0:27.note: The Hebrew word for "shattered" echoes the breaking of idols and pottery in judgment scenes, linking rebellion to self-destruction.

0:28.rendering: For they will be ashamed of the sacred oaks that you have delighted in, and you will be embarrassed by the gardens that you have chosen.
0:28.note: The Hebrew word for "sacred oaks" echoes the name of the first pagan shrine Abraham visited in Genesis 12:6, turning Israel's idolatry into a full-circle failure of their ancestral promise.

0:29.rendering: For you will be like an oak whose leaf withers, and like a garden that has no water.
0:29.note: The withering oak and waterless garden echo the garden of Eden's loss and the curses of Deuteronomy 28, painting covenant unfaithfulness as self-chosen desolation.

0:30.rendering: The strong will become tinder, and his work a spark. Both will burn together, with no one to quench them.
0:30.note: The Hebrew word for "strong" (chason) puns on the idea of wealth and power, while "work" (poal) hints at what the powerful produce, both reduced to fuel for unquenchable fire.

1:0.rendering: This is what Isaiah son of Amoz saw concerning Judah and Jerusalem.
1:0.note: The word "saw" here is the same Hebrew verb used for prophetic visions, hinting that this oracle is itself a kind of divine panorama unfolding across the coming chapters.

1:1.rendering: In the last days the mountain of the LORD's house will be established as the highest of the mountains, lifted up above the hills, and all the nations will stream to it.
1:1.note: This vision of the mountain draws on ancient Near Eastern imagery of sacred peaks, now transformed into a magnet pulling the world's peoples toward God's presence.

1:2.rendering: Many peoples will come and say, "Come, let us go up to the mountain of the LORD, to the house of the God of Jacob. He will teach us his ways, and we will walk in his paths." For the law will go out from Zion, and the word of the LORD from Jerusalem.
1:2.note: This vision of nations streaming to Zion for instruction echoes the promise to Abraham that all families of the earth would be blessed through his offspring.

1:3.rendering: He will judge between the nations, and settle disputes for many peoples. They will beat their swords into plow blades, and their spears into pruning hooks. Nation will not lift sword against nation, nor will they train for war anymore.
1:3.note: This vision of weapons reforged for farming echoes the ancient hope that true justice turns tools of death into instruments of life.

1:4.rendering: House of Jacob, come, let us walk in the light of the LORD.
1:4.note: This urgent invitation echoes the nations' pilgrimage to Zion in verses 2-4, turning the vision outward into a call for Israel itself to live the light it is meant to share.

1:5.rendering: For you have abandoned your people, the house of Jacob, because they are filled with eastern ways, and they practice divination like the Philistines, and they clasp hands with foreigners.
1:5.note: The Hebrew word for "abandoned" echoes God's heartbreaking verdict over his people, mirroring how he once forsook the land in judgment yet always hints at future restoration.

1:6.rendering: Their land is filled with silver and gold, and there is no end to their treasures. Their land is filled with horses, and there is no end to their chariots.
1:6.note: The doubled "no end" echoes the endless abundance God promised Abraham's descendants, now twisted into self-reliant military might.

1:7.rendering: Their land is filled with idols. They bow down to the work of their own hands, to what their own fingers have made.
1:7.note: The Hebrew word for "idols" (elilim) puns on "nothingness," exposing handmade gods as empty puffs of nothing.

1:8.rendering: So humanity is bowed down, each one brought low, and you must not forgive them.
1:8.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "bow down" and "brought low" echoes the humbling of the proud in verse 11, showing how idolatry reduces people to the dust they worship.

1:9.rendering: Enter into the rock, and hide yourself in the dust, from before the terror of the Lord and from the splendor of his majesty.
1:9.note: The doubled Hebrew phrase for "terror of the Lord and splendor of his majesty" echoes exactly in verses 19 and 21, forming a refrain that drives the whole chapter.

1:10.rendering: The eyes of the arrogant will be brought low, and the pride of men will be humbled. Yahweh alone will be exalted on that day.
1:10.note: The Hebrew wordplay links "brought low" (shaphel) with "humbled" (shachach), echoing the judgment on the serpent in Genesis 3:14.

1:11.rendering: For the LORD of hosts has a day against all that is proud and lifted up, against all that is high, so that it will be brought low.
1:11.note: The repeated Hebrew word for "high" or "lifted up" echoes the tower of Babel and the serpent's promise in Eden, exposing every false claim to exaltation.

1:12.rendering: He is against all the cedars of Lebanon, the lofty and lifted up ones, and against all the oaks of Bashan.
1:12.note: The towering cedars and oaks here echo the proud, high things God opposes in verse 12, using the tallest trees of the ancient world to picture human arrogance that will be felled.

1:13.rendering: against all the high mountains, and against all the lofty hills,
1:13.note: This verse echoes the ancient motif of mountains as symbols of arrogant human power, now leveled before the Lord's day.

1:14.rendering: against every high tower and against every fortified wall
1:14.note: Isaiah stacks these images like a siege list, echoing the tower of Babel's proud heights now brought low.

1:15.rendering: against all the ships of Tarshish, and against all the beautiful vessels of delight.
1:15.note: This verse echoes the very ships Solomon used to bring gold and exotic goods, now listed among the proud things God will bring low.

1:16.rendering: The haughtiness of humans will be brought low, and the pride of men will be humbled. The LORD alone will be exalted on that day.
1:16.note: This verse echoes Isaiah 2:11 almost word for word, hammering home the theme that human arrogance must fall so the LORD alone stands high.

1:17.note: This terse Hebrew line uses a rare verb for "pass away" that echoes the flood's total removal of wickedness in Genesis 7, hinting that the day of the Lord will sweep away every rival to the Creator.

1:18.rendering: People will go into caves in the rocks and holes in the ground from the terror of the LORD and the splendor of his majesty, when he rises to terrify the earth.
1:18.note: The same Hebrew phrase for "terror of the LORD and splendor of his majesty" echoes back to Isaiah 2:10, forming a literary bracket around this section.

1:19.rendering: In that day, people will throw their idols of silver and their idols of gold, which they made for themselves to worship, to the moles and to the bats.
1:19.note: This verse echoes Isaiah's earlier condemnation of human-made idols, showing how even precious metals crafted by skilled hands become worthless trash when the Lord alone is exalted.

1:20.rendering: to go into the crevices of the rocks and into the clefts of the cliffs, from before the terror of the Lord and from the splendor of his majesty, when he rises to terrify the earth.
1:20.note: This verse echoes the exact hiding places in Isaiah 2:19, bookending the list of panicked flight like a poetic refrain.

1:21.rendering: Stop trusting in mere humans, who have only a breath in their nostrils. Of what account are they?
1:21.note: The Hebrew word for breath, neshamah, echoes the creation of humanity in Genesis 2:7, underscoring how fragile human power is.
